fact: October 31 is exactly 61 days after the autumn equinox, making it the perfect middle point between fall starting and winter arriving in the Northern Hemisphere!
Bats are typically at their fattest right now! They've been gorging on insects all month to build up fat reserves before hibernating through winter.
In the Southern Hemisphere, October 31 marks the beginning of their hottest season - it's like their version of late May, with flowers blooming everywhere!
The sun usually sets before 6 PM across most of the United States on this date, and by November 3rd, it'll be setting before 5 PM in many places thanks to the end of daylight saving time.

Spiders are actually dying off by October 31st in cold climates - most of the creepy crawlers you see are already dead or about to freeze, making this ironically one of the least spidery times of year!
Tree sap typically stops flowing completely around this date in temperate regions, which is why maple syrup season ended months ago and won't start again until late winter.
October 31st falls during the peak migration time for monarch butterflies - millions of them are often flying through Mexico right now on their way to their winter homes!
The moon rises about 50 minutes later each night, and on October 31st, this creates some of the most dramatic moonrise colors of the year as it climbs through the thick autumn atmosphere.
Squirrels bury up to 10,000 nuts during October, and by Halloween, they've typically forgotten where they put about 75% of them - those lost nuts will grow into new trees!
In northern Alaska, October 31st marks the beginning of polar night in some areas, where the sun won't rise again for over 60 days straight!
Deer are usually in the middle of their breeding season right now, making them extra bold and active - they're so distracted by romance that they wander into places they normally avoid!
